docs.title: Directed Evolution Monitoring Web Portal
docs.text:
User Manual

Welcome to the User Manual for the Directed Evolution Monitoring Web Portal — a computational platform designed to support the tracking, analysis, and visualisation of data generated from droplet-based microfluidics directed evolution experiments, specifically targeting DNA polymerase optimisation.

Use the navigation on the left to get started.

docs.title: Staging and Validation
docs.text:
What UniProt accession ID format should I use?

Use the standard UniProt accession ID format (e.g. P05067, Q9Y2T1), which can be found by searching for your protein on the UniProt website. Gene names, entry names, or isoform identifiers are not accepted.

Do I need to select a start codon when starting a new experiment?

Selecting the start codon that corresponds to your expression system may reduce computational runtime. ATG is the canonical start codon in most systems. GTG and TTG are alternative start codons found in prokaryotes such as E. coli. If unsure, leave all three unselected and the system will automatically consider all three during ORF identification.

The experimental staging succeeded, but the alignment score is low. Is this concerning?

A score above 0.85 is accepted as a valid match. Any score between 0.85 and 0.95 indicates minor sequence differences between the canonical UniProt reference protein and the protein encoded in the plasmid. This might be expected if the plasmid encodes a mutated or engineered variant of the wild-type protein.

My alignment score is very low and experimental staging failed. What does this mean?

A score below 0.85 is not accepted as a valid match. This may be due to an incorrect UniProt accession ID, an incorrect plasmid FASTA, a mismatched start codon selection, or a highly divergent sequence. Please verify all of these before retrying.

docs.title: Data Upload and Quality Control
docs.text:
Could the system support other file formats?

No — the portal currently only accepts FASTA files for plasmid upload, and TSV or JSON files for experimental data. However, since the parsing module uses Pandas, extending support to other formats such as CSV or Excel could be added in future versions if required.

Can I upload data from multiple generations in one file?

Yes — the portal expects a single TSV or JSON file containing all generations. The system automatically groups variants by generation using the Directed_Evolution_Generation column.

Can I upload experimental data with additional metadata columns?

The parser is designed with a flexible schema, so additional metadata columns do not cause errors or affect analysis. Only the seven essential fields are validated and used for analysis, while any extra columns are preserved in the database for potential future use.

What happens to rows that fail quality control?

Rows with missing values in essential fields are excluded from analysis and are not stored in the database. The remaining dataset proceeds to variant activity analysis normally.

docs.title: Activity Score and Results
docs.text:
What does an Activity Score of 1.0 mean?

A score of 1.0 means the variant performed equivalently to the wildtype control for that generation. Scores above 1.0 indicate improved catalytic activity, and scores below 1.0 indicate reduced activity relative to the wildtype control.

Why would a variant with a high Activity Score have low protein abundance?

The Activity Score is calculated as DNA yield divided by protein yield, normalised to the wildtype control mean. A variant with low protein expression but proportionally high DNA output will score highly, reflecting efficient catalytic activity per unit of protein produced rather than high expression.

What does clustering in the 3D PCA landscape mean?

Clustering of high-activity variants in the same region of the landscape suggests those variants share similar sequence features associated with improved performance. This can help identify which regions of sequence space are worth targeting in future mutagenesis rounds.

docs.title: Future Improvements
docs.text:
The following enhancements are planned for future versions of the portal:

Configurable Activity Metric: The current Activity Score pipeline is hardcoded to use DNA yield and protein abundance as the efficiency ratio, limiting the portal to DNA polymerase directed evolution applications. Future development would allow users to specify which experimental measurement columns map to the expected catalytic output when initialising a new experiment. This would extend the portal's applicability to a wider range of enzyme classes without requiring changes to the quality control, or visualisation components of the pipeline.

Offline UniProt caching: To remove the dependency on an active internet connection during staging, future versions will implement a local cache of previously retrieved UniProt records. This would allow experiments referencing previously used accession IDs to be staged without requiring a live API call, improving reliability in restricted or offline network environments.

Adaptive alignment threshold: Rather than a fixed staging threshold, future versions will implement an adaptive strategy that adjusts based on sequence length and divergence, improving compatibility with a wider range of plasmid constructs.

Alternative genetic code support: Future versions will allow users to select a codon table when initialising an experiment, extending support to organisms and expression systems that use non-standard genetic codes.

Enhanced Activity Score modelling: Planned improvements include log-transformed scaling, confidence interval estimation, and outlier detection to produce more statistically robust activity measurements.

Replicate-aware analysis: Future development will include the ability to upload and aggregate replicate measurements, calculate variance, and display error estimates alongside activity scores.

Improved computational performance: Optimisations are planned to support larger variant libraries and reduce processing times for high-throughput datasets.

Machine learning–assisted variant ranking: Future versions may incorporate predictive models trained on mutation patterns to help identify high-performing variants and detect epistatic interactions before experimental validation.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
The Directed Evolution Analysis Web Portal is designed to support the tracking, analysis, and visualisation of data generated from droplet-based microfluidics directed evolution (DE) experiments, aimed to specifically optimise DNA polymerases.

Directed evolution workflows often produce large numbers of variants across multiple generations, making it difficult to efficiently interpret experimental results without computational support.

This portal provides a simple interface that allows users to stage experiments, upload experimental datasets, analyse variant activity, and visualise evolutionary trends. By automating these steps, the system helps transform raw experimental measurements into interpretable results that support biological decision-making within the Design–Build–Test–Learn (DBTL) cycle.

This document serves as the User Manual and provides instructions on how to access and use the web portal. Detailed explanations of the internal architecture, database design, and computational methods used in the system are described separately in the Technical Documentation.

This portal is intended for researchers working with directed evolution experiments, including:

  • Synthetic biologists
  • Protein engineers
  • Molecular biologists
  • Computational biologists analysing variant libraries

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
The portal provides the following capabilities:

  • Experiment staging and plasmid validation against a UniProt reference protein
  • Automated parsing and quality control of experimental datasets
  • Variant activity analysis using a unified Activity Score (using DNA yield / Protein yield)
  • Identification of mutations relative to the wild-type reference (synonymous, non-synonymous, truncating, insertions, deletions)
  • Visualisation of experimental results through a ranked top 10 best performing table and activity plots (Activity Distribution per Generation Plot and 3D PCA-Based Activity Landscape)
  • Generation of downloadable reports summarising analysis results

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
The portal is designed to support directed evolution experiments, but the following limitations should be kept in mind when interpreting results:

Activity Score metric is specific to DNA polymerase engineering: The Activity Score is currently formulated specifically for DNA polymerase directed evolution experiments, where DNA yield is a direct proxy for catalytic activity. The metric assumes that DNA yield reflects polymerase enzymatic activity, and that normalising by protein yield isolates the catalytic efficiency from expression-level differences. This metric would not be appropriate for other types of enzyme or molecule, which would require a different output measurement to reflect catalytic activity. The portal is therefore not currently generalisable to directed evolution experiments targeting other enzyme types.

Internet connectivity required: The portal requires an active internet connection during experiment staging to retrieve protein sequence and annotation data from the UniProt REST API. Staging cannot be completed in offline environments, and UniProt server unavailability may cause staging to fail. If this occurs, retry again once connectivity is restored.

Staging alignment threshold: The staging pipeline uses a fixed similarity threshold to validate whether the uploaded plasmid encodes the target protein. In rare cases involving highly divergent or heavily engineered target sequences, the system may not successfully match a valid ORF. If staging fails unexpectedly, verify that the correct UniProt accession ID and plasmid FASTA have been provided.

Standard genetic code only: The portal currently assumes the standard genetic code during translation. Alternative genetic codes used by specific organisms or organelles are not supported. Results may be unreliable if your expression system uses a non-standard codon table.

Activity Score is a comparative metric: The Activity Score reflects relative catalytic performance normalised to the wildtype control and is best used for ranking and comparing variants within the same experiment. It is not a mechanistic model of enzyme activity and does not include statistical confidence estimates.

No replicate support: The system does not currently aggregate replicate measurements or calculate variance and error estimates across replicates. Experimental uploading, and the final results should be interpreted with this in mind when drawing statistical conclusions.

Mutation classification is sequence-based: Mutations are classified as synonymous, nonsynonymous, truncating, based on sequence comparison alone. Structural effects, protein stability impacts, and domain-level functional consequences are not currently predicted.

Performance on large datasets: The portal is suited to moderate experimental datasets. Analysis of very large variant libraries may result in increased processing times.

docs.title: 1. Create an Account
docs.text:
1. Click Register/Login
  2. Enter your email, username, and password
  3. Submit the form

docs.title: 2. Starting a New Experiment
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the Experiments page using the top menu
  2. Click Create New Experiment
  3. Enter an experiment name
  4. Enter a UniProt accession ID
  5. Upload your plasmid FASTA file
  6. Specify the expected start codon (optional)
  7. Create New Experiment

Important

  • Use a valid UniProt accession ID (e.g. P05067)
  • Only one plasmid FASTA file is allowed per experiment
  • If no start codon is selected, the system will default to considering all three: ATG, GTG, and TTG

docs.title: 3. ORF Staging/Validation Phase
docs.text:
The system will automatically:

  1. Retrieve the UniProt record and store the protein sequence and key domain annotations
  2. Validate the plasmid FASTA sequence
  3. Detect candidate ORFs across all six reading frames
  4. Select the best ORF by alignment against the UniProt reference protein
  5. Return the match status (match or no_match)

docs.title: 4. Upload Directed Evolution Data
docs.text:
Upload your directed evolution experimental dataset (TSV or JSON format). The file must contain the following required columns:

  • Plasmid_Variant_Index
  • Parent_Plasmid_Variant
  • Directed_Evolution_Generation
  • Assembled_DNA_Sequence
  • DNA_Quantification_fg
  • Protein_Quantification_pg
  • Control

docs.title: 5. View Results
docs.text:
Once analysis is complete, the following outputs are generated:

Top 10 Ranked Variant Table — Lists the highest-performing variants ranked by their Activity Score, allowing quick identification of variants with improved catalytic performance compared to the wild-type enzyme.

Activity Distribution per Generation — Shows how Activity Scores are distributed across each generation, helping users observe whether overall activity improves, decreases, or stabilises over successive rounds of selection.

3D Activity Landscape — Maps variants in sequence space and displays their Activity Scores as a three-dimensional surface, helping users explore how sequence diversity relates to functional performance.

Downloadable PDF Report — The complete analysis report, including all tables and visualisations, can be downloaded as a PDF for record keeping or further analysis.

docs.title: 1. Software Requirements
docs.text:
• Python ≥ 3.9
  • Active internet connection (required for UniProt retrieval)

The system relies on several Python libraries for data processing, sequence analysis, and visualisation. These dependencies are listed in the requirements.txt file and must be installed before running the application.

Library Purpose Flask Web application framework for routing and the user interface SQLAlchemy Object-relational mapping for database interaction Pandas Data parsing and quality control for experimental datasets Biopython Biological sequence handling, translation, and alignment Requests Retrieval of protein records from the UniProt API NumPy Numerical operations used during analysis and visualisation Matplotlib Static plotting for activity distribution graphs Plotly Interactive 3D visualisations of the activity landscape Scikit-learn PCA dimensionality reduction and K-Nearest Neighbour regression

docs.title: 2. Installation
docs.text:
Step 1. Clone the repository: 

git clone https://github.com/sumaiyazainab/group-project-monetery-.git

Step 2. Install dependencies: 

pip install -r requirements.txt

Step 3. Run the application: 

flask run

Step 4. Open your browser and navigate to: 

http://127.0.0.1:5000

docs.title: 3. Supported Data Formats
docs.text: Input Format Extension Plasmid sequence FASTA .fasta or .fa Experimental data TSV .tsv Experimental data JSON .json

docs.title: UniProt and Staging Issues
docs.text: Issue Error Message Troubleshooting Steps UniProt accession not found ValueError: The UniProt accession 'X' does not exist... (HTTP 404) Confirm the accession ID is correct (e.g. P05067). Search for the target protein on UniProt manually and copy the accession ID. UniProt accession format invalid ValueError: The UniProt ID format you entered is invalid... (HTTP 400) Ensure you are not using a gene name instead of an accession. Use canonical accession format (e.g. Q9Y2T1) and remove any extra spaces. UniProt server error RuntimeError: UniProt server error. Please try again later. (5xx) Retry later. Verify internet access and check UniProt REST availability. Request timed out RuntimeError: We could not connect to UniProt in time... Check your network connectivity and try again. Network/connection error RuntimeError: An unexpected network error occurred... Confirm your device has internet access. Disable any VPN or proxy if present. UniProt record missing sequence RuntimeError: The UniProt record is missing a protein sequence. Verify the accession ID and confirm a protein sequence is displayed on the UniProt website. If the issue persists, the UniProt REST API structure may have changed — report the issue.

docs.title: FASTA and ORF Issues
docs.text: Issue Error Message Troubleshooting Steps FASTA contains multiple sequences ValueError: There is more than one plasmid sequence in the FASTA file... Ensure the uploaded FASTA contains exactly one record (one > header line and one continuous sequence). FASTA contains zero sequences ValueError: No plasmid sequence was found in the FASTA file... Ensure the file is a valid FASTA, starts with a > header, and contains sequence lines. Invalid DNA characters ValueError: Invalid characters found in DNA sequence: [...] Remove any non-ACGTN characters (e.g. numbers, whitespace, or IUPAC ambiguity codes beyond N). Convert sequence to A/C/G/T/N only. No ORFs returned No ORFs returned Verify the plasmid contains a coding region and double-check the start codon selection. No ORF matches UniProt Status = no_match_found Confirm the correct UniProt accession ID and plasmid have been provided and are compatible.

docs.title: Creating a New Account
docs.text: To create a new account, enter your desired username, email address, and password, then click Register to complete registration. Once registered, you can log in using your username and password going forward.

docs.title: Logging In
docs.text: To log in to your account, enter your unique username and password and click Login.

docs.title: Viewing Experiment History
docs.text:
After logging in, click Experiments in the top menu bar to see a list of all previously run experiments. Each experiment displays one of the following status indicators:

Status Meaning Staged Experiment successfully validated and ready for data upload Analysis Complete Analysis completed successfully Error An error occurred during staging or analysis

Click an experiment name to view its full details.

Create a new experiment by clicking Create New Experiment.

docs.title: Logging Out
docs.text: To log out, press Logout in the top menu bar.

docs.title: Managing Experiments
docs.text:
After logging in, to view past experiments, or to create a new experiment, navigate to the dashboard page or directly to the experiments page by clicking Dashboard or Experiments in the top menu bar.

The following experiment details are displayed:

  • Experiment Name
  • Status
  • Message
  • Selected start codon
  • Date and time the experiment was created

Click an experiment name to view its full details and results.

docs.title: Creating a New Experiment and Running Staging/Validation
docs.text:
1. Clicking the Create New Experiment button in the Experiments page or on the Dashboard page brings you to this page
  2. Provide an experiment name
  3. Provide a UniProt accession ID for your target protein
  4. Upload the wildtype plasmid FASTA file
  5. Specify the expected start codon (optional, but will improve runtimes)
  6. Click Create Experiment
  7. You will be directed to the home page where you will need to navigate back to the Experiment page using the top menu bar to see your new Experiment
  8. You should see the newly created experiment, with a pending status (Keep in mind this step does not automatically begin the staging/validation process)
  9. Click into the newly created experiment name, then click Run UniProt/Plasmid Validation to run the staging/validation process
  10. You should be able to see the results immediately, indicated by a change in status in the Experiment Detail table:
Status Message What it means Match Found Validation successful, move on to data upload and analysis The plasmid ORF has been successfully matched to the UniProt reference protein — you can now upload your experimental data No Match Found No suitable match found, double-check your FASTA file and UniProt accession The staging pipeline could not match any ORF to the reference protein — verify your inputs and try again

Start Codon Selection

Select the start codon that corresponds to your expression system:

  • ATG — standard for most organisms
  • GTG — common in prokaryotic systems such as E. coli
  • TTG — common in prokaryotic systems such as E. coli

If you are unsure, leave all three selected to allow the system to detect any of the three automatically.

docs.title: Mutation Annotation and Variant Activity Analysis
Mutation Annotation
docs.text:
Variant sequences are compared to the validated wild-type reference sequence to identify mutations. The system classifies mutations into the following categories:

  • Synonymous mutations — nucleotide changes that do not alter the amino acid sequence
  • Non-synonymous mutations — nucleotide changes that alter the encoded amino acid
  • Truncating mutations — mutations that introduce premature stop codons

These mutation statistics help researchers understand how sequence variation may influence enzyme function.

docs.title: Activity Score
docs.text:
The Activity Score is a normalised metric that represents the intrinsic catalytic performance of each DNA polymerase variant, independent of expression level or batch variability.

How it is calculated: Step 1: Activity is calculated by DNA/Protein ratio per variant Step 2: Normalise to per-generation wildtype control mean

docs.title: How It Is Calculated
docs.text:
Step 1 — Raw activity per variant: 

Raw Activity = DNA_Quantification_fg / Protein_Quantification_pg

This estimates how much DNA output was produced per unit of protein for each variant.

Step 2 — Normalise to per-generation wildtype control mean: 

Activity Score = Raw Activity (variant) / Mean Raw Activity (WT controls, same generation as the variant)

The per-generation wildtype controls are the rows marked Control = True in the uploaded experimental data file. Each generation's variants are normalised to their own generation's wildtype mean to account for batch effects and assay drift between rounds.

docs.title: Activity Score Interpretation
docs.text:
Score Interpretation Above 1.0 Improved catalytic activity relative to wildtype ≈ 1.0 Equivalent activity to wildtype Below 1.0 Reduced activity relative to wildtype

For example, an Activity Score of 2.0 means the variant produces twice as much DNA per unit protein as the wildtype control in that generation, suggesting improved polymerase efficiency rather than simply increased expression.

docs.title: Biological Justification for the Activity Score
docs.text:
Raw DNA yield alone does not accurately represent enzymatic performance because variants may differ in protein expression levels. A variant that produces more enzymes could generate more DNA without necessarily having improved catalytic efficiency. By normalising DNA production by protein abundance, the Activity Score estimates the intrinsic catalytic efficiency of the polymerase, independent of expression level.

Important Notes

  • The Activity Score is a comparative ranking metric — it is designed for ranking and comparing variants within and across generations of the same experiment. Direct comparison across separate experiments should be done with caution.
  • The metric assumes a linear relationship between DNA output and enzymatic activity. Non-linear biological effects are not currently modelled.

docs.title: Uploading Experimental Data
docs.text: The Experimental Data Parsing & Quality Control is first carried out to ensure that downstream analysis is only carried out on scientifically valid data. This also prevents potential silent failures of the analysis pipeline.

docs.title: 1. Navigating to the Experimental Data Upload Page
docs.text: From the Experimental Details and Validation Results page, click Upload Experimental Data at the bottom of the page to be directed to the Upload Experimental Data page

docs.title: 2. How to Upload the Experimental Data
docs.text:
1. Click Choose file and select your file
  2. Click Upload to upload the file

  3. You will be automatically redirected back to the Experiment Details and Validation Results page. The Experiment Detail status and message should be now:

    Status Message What it means Data Uploaded X variants accepted, X variants rejected Experimental data has been parsed and quality checked, and the number of valid and rejected rows are shown

  4. Click Run Full Analysis to begin the analysis process (runtime: ~2 minutes)

  5. Once the analysis is completed, the Experimental Detail status and message changes to:

    Status Message What it means Analysis Complete Analysis completed successfully All variants have been annotated and scored — results are now available to view

  6. Results and visualisations will appear in the same page, underneath Experiment Details and Validation Results

docs.title: 3. Experimental Data File Formats
docs.text:
The system only supports the following formats:

Format Extension Description TSV .tsv Tab-separated experimental data JSON .json Structured JSON experimental data

Uploading any other file type will result in an error, and both formats must contain all required essential fields (see below).

docs.title: 4. Required Data Fields
docs.text:
The uploaded file must contain the following essential columns:

  • Plasmid_Variant_Index
  • Parent_Plasmid_Variant
  • Directed_Evolution_Generation
  • Assembled_DNA_Sequence
  • DNA_Quantification_fg
  • Protein_Quantification_pg
  • Control

If any of these columns are missing, the upload will be rejected. Additional metadata columns beyond these seven are accepted and stored without affecting analysis.

docs.title: 5. Parsing and Quality Control
docs.text:
Once the file is uploaded and analysis is running, the system automatically performs the following checks:

1. File Type Detection — The parser determines whether the file is TSV or JSON.

2. Column-Level Validation — Ensures all essential fields are present in the file.

3. Row-Level Validation — Checks each row for missing critical values. Rows that lack essential data (e.g. missing variant index, DNA quantification, protein quantification, or DNA sequence) are rejected and excluded from analysis.

docs.title: 6. Data Storage
docs.text:
After Quality Control:

  • Valid rows are inserted into the SQL database
  • Rejected rows are excluded from analysis
  • Data is linked to the authenticated user and the specific experiment

This ensures data segregation and scientific integrity.

docs.title: 7. Why Quality Control is Important
docs.text:
Accurate DNA and protein quantification values are essential for calculating the Activity Score. The QC module rejects incomplete or corrupted entries to ensure:

  • Reliable mutation classification
  • Accurate Activity Score calculation
  • Meaningful visualisation outputs
  • Reproducible scientific results

docs.title: 8. Quick Data Upload Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Action Missing essential columns error Ensure required column headers are correctly named Unsupported file type Upload only .tsv or .json files Rows rejected Check for missing values in essential fields

docs.title: Understanding Validation Results
docs.text: After staging, the portal displays the validation status, match or no match, in the Experimental Details section:

docs.title: Match or No Match Status
docs.text:
Status Message What it means Match Found Validation successful, move on to data upload and analysis The plasmid ORF has been successfully matched to the UniProt reference protein — you can now upload your experimental data No Match Found No suitable match found, double-check your FASTA file and UniProt accession The staging pipeline could not match any ORF to the reference protein — verify your inputs and try again

More detailed information about the staging results are displayed underneath in the Validation Results section:

docs.title: Validation Results
docs.text:
Field Description Best ORF Start Position The nucleotide position within the plasmid where the best-matching ORF begins Alignment Score The score fraction (0–1) reflecting how closely the best candidate ORF matches the UniProt reference protein. A score ≥ 0.85 is required for successful staging WT Protein Length The amino acid length of the best-matched ORF translated sequence UniProt Protein Length The amino acid length of the reference protein retrieved from UniProt

Tip

The WT Protein Length and UniProt Protein Length should be similar if the correct ORF has been identified. A large difference may indicate that the wrong plasmid or accession ID was provided.

docs.title: Extracted Key Domains
docs.text:
The portal also displays a table of key functional domains extracted from the UniProt record for your target protein. These annotations are retrieved automatically during staging alongside the reference protein sequence from UniProt.

Column Description Type The type of annotated feature — all entries shown here are Domain annotations Description The name of the functional domain (e.g. 5'-3' exonuclease, 3'-5' exonuclease) Start Position The amino acid position where the domain begins in the reference protein sequence End Position The amino acid position where the domain ends in the reference protein sequence

This information is useful for interpreting mutation results — mutations that fall within a known functional domain are more likely to affect enzyme activity than those in non-annotated regions.

docs.title: If Staging Returns a Match: Validation successful
docs.text: If the validation result shows succesful match found, move on to the next step by clicking Upload Experimental Data at the bottom of the page to upload your TSV/JSON experimental data.

docs.title: If Staging Returns No Match Found: Validation unsuccessful
docs.text: If the experiment status shows No Match Found, this means the portal was unable to find a candidate ORF within the uploaded plasmid that sufficiently matches the UniProt reference protein.

docs.title: How the Match Threshold Works
docs.text:
The portal compares each candidate ORF against the UniProt reference protein using a similarity score. A match is only accepted if the alignment score reaches a minimum threshold of 0.85 out of 1.0. This threshold is designed to:

  • Allow for moderate sequence differences between the plasmid construct and the reference protein, such as mutations or small insertions and deletions
  • Reject ORFs that are unrelated to the target protein
  • Reduce false positives from large random ORFs that may partially align by chance

If no candidate ORF reaches this threshold, the experiment status is set to No Match Found and staging cannot proceed.

docs.title: What to Check
docs.text:
If you receive a No Match Found status, verify the following before re-attempting staging:

Check Action Incorrect UniProt accession ID Confirm the accession ID matches your target protein on the UniProt website (uniprot.org) Wrong plasmid FASTA uploaded Verify the uploaded FASTA file contains the correct plasmid sequence encoding your target gene Mismatched start codon If your gene uses GTG or TTG and only ATG was selected, try enabling all three start codons Highly divergent sequence If the plasmid encodes a heavily engineered variant, the sequence similarity may fall below the threshold even with correct inputs

If you have verified all of the above and staging continues to fail, refer to the Troubleshooting Guide for further steps.

docs.title: Visualisations
docs.text:
Once the analysis is done, the status and message within the Experimental Details table should show:

Status Message What it means Analysis Complete Analysis completed successfully All variants have been annotated and scored — results are now available to view

To complement the numerical ranking, the portal generates several visualisations that provide different perspectives on the experimental results. These visualisations help researchers interpret both individual variant performance and population-level evolutionary trends.

The analysis report includes three primary outputs: - Top 10 Variant Table - Activity Distribution per Generation Plot - 3D PCA-Based Activity Landscape

docs.title: Top 10 Variant Table
docs.text:
After the analysis is complete, the results page displays a Top 10 Variant Table showing the highest-performing variants identified in the experiment. The table ranks variants according to their Activity Score, with the best-performing variant appearing at the top. Each row represents one variant from the experiment.

Example table:

Description Rank Position of the variant based on Activity Score Variant Identifier (Plasmid Variant Index) Unique identifier for the variant in the dataset Parent Variant The variant from the previous generation that this variant was derived from Directed Evolution Generation The generation in which the variant was produced DNA Quantification Measured DNA yield for that variant Protein Quantification Measured protein abundance for that variant Activity Score Normalised metric representing catalytic performance relative to the wild-type control Mutation Counts Number of mutations compared to the wild-type sequence, broken down by synonymous, non-synonymous, and truncating mutations

By reviewing this table, users can quickly identify variants that show improved catalytic activity compared to the wild-type control. These variants are often strong candidates for further experimental validation or for use as parent sequences in subsequent rounds of directed evolution.

docs.title: Activity Distribution per Generation Plot
docs.text:
The Activity Distribution plot shows how Activity Scores are distributed across different generations of the directed evolution experiment. This visualisation helps users understand how variant performance changes throughout the directed evolution experiment. Each generation is represented by a violin-shaped distribution summarising the Activity Scores of all variants belonging to that generation.

Example plot:

Each violin displays the following features:

Feature Description Distribution shape The width of the violin indicates how many variants fall within a particular Activity Score range Mean activity value The average Activity Score for variants in that generation Median activity value The central value representing the midpoint of the distribution Spread of activity values The variation in performance between variants within that generation

By examining this plot, users can observe whether variant activity improves, stabilises, or declines across successive generations. For example, if the overall distribution shifts toward higher Activity Scores in later generations, this suggests that the directed evolution process is successfully enriching for improved variants.

The diagram below shows an example output generated from a sample dataset, illustrating the key patterns users should look for when analysing their own experimental results.

docs.title: 3D PCA-Based Activity Landscape
docs.text:
The 3D Activity Landscape provides a visual overview of how sequence variation relates to functional performance across all variants in the experiment.

Variants are mapped into a two-dimensional sequence space using principal component analysis (PCA) based on protein sequence features. The Activity Score of each variant is then represented along the vertical axis, creating a three-dimensional surface.

Example plot:

Each visualisation displays the following features:

Feature Description Variant points Each point represents an individual variant from the dataset Height of the surface The vertical axis reflects the Activity Score of each variant Peaks Regions of high activity where variants show improved catalytic performance Valleys Regions of low activity representing poorly performing variants

This visualisation helps users explore how sequence changes relate to functional performance. Variants that cluster together in sequence space often share similar mutation patterns, while peaks in the landscape may highlight high-performing variants that could be selected for further experimental investigation or future rounds of directed evolution.

The diagram below shows an example output generated from a sample dataset.

docs.title: Downloading the Report
docs.text: Once analysis is complete, the full results page can be downloaded as a PDF from the results page.
